title: The Unknown Planet
description: Discover Bayesian inference through exploration of an unknown planet. Learn that beliefs sharpen with data following a 1/√N law, derive Bayes' theorem from conditional probability, and verify your derivations numerically.

blocks.title: The Unknown Planet
blocks.content_html:
A planet floats in the center of the screen, shrouded in clouds. You can only learn about it by tossing a ball: where it lands, a dot appears. Blue = water, Brown = land. Each toss reveals one pixel of truth.

What will you discover about how beliefs sharpen with data?
blocks.instructions:
1. Click 'Toss' to reveal dots on the planet.
2. Watch the belief curve update after each toss.
3. Observe how the curve changes as you collect more data.
4. Try different toss counts (1, 10, 100) to explore faster.
blocks.canvas_prompt: Write on the canvas: what happens to the belief curve as you toss more? What happens to its height and width? Write at least two observations.

blocks.title: The Power of the Prior
blocks.content_html: Now you can control your starting belief. Set it far from the truth and watch what happens.
blocks.instructions:
1. Set your starting belief far from what you think is true.
2. Toss 10 times.
3. Toss 100 more times.
4. Watch when the starting belief stops mattering.
blocks.canvas_prompt: Write on the canvas: when does the starting belief stop mattering? At what point does the data overwhelm your initial assumption?

blocks.title: Predict the Width
blocks.prompt: With a flat starting belief and 9 tosses (6 water, 3 land), the belief curve's 89% interval has width ≈ 0.35.\n\nNow imagine 9× more data: 81 tosses with the same 2/3 water rate (54 water, 27 land). Predict the new width of the 89% interval.

error_patterns.feedback: Good — you knew more data means a narrower belief. But when N went from 9 to 27 (3× more data), did the width shrink to 1/3? The width shrinks by 1/√N, not 1/N.

error_patterns.diagnosis: May have divided by 81 instead of √81
error_patterns.feedback: The actual factor for 9× more data is √9 = 3, not 9 or 81. Width shrinks much slower than you predicted.
More data always sharpens belief. Go back to the simulation and observe.
Strong intuition! The answer is ≈0.12. Width ∝ 1/√N.

blocks.title: Test Your Prediction
blocks.content_html: Now see what actually happens with 81 tosses.
blocks.instructions: Click 'Toss' to run 81 tosses with 54 water. The actual width is 0.12.

blocks.title: Find the Pattern
blocks.prompt: Collect data at N = 3, 9, 27, 81, 243 (always with 2/3 water rate). Record the 89% interval width at each size. Can you spot the pattern?
blocks.canvas_prompt: Each time N triples, the width changes by a factor. What factor? Write what you notice.
blocks.discovery_text: Width × √N is nearly constant. Width ≈ C/√N, where C ≈ 1.0. 9× more data doesn't make your belief 9× sharper — it makes it √9 = 3× sharper.

blocks.title: New Notation
blocks.content_html:
New notation: We write P(A|B) to mean "the probability of A given that B is true." The vertical bar | means "given."

We'll use these terms:

  • p — the fraction of the planet that's water (what we're trying to learn)
  • data — the sequence of tosses we've observed
  • P(p | data) — our belief about p after seeing the data (the belief curve)
  • P(data | p) — the probability of getting this data if we knew p

blocks.title: Derive Bayes' Theorem (Part A)
blocks.prompt: Derive Bayes' theorem from the definition of conditional probability. Write each step on the canvas.

steps.instruction: Write the definition of conditional probability
steps.explanation: This is our starting point — the definition of conditional probability.

steps.instruction: Rewrite P(p ∩ data) using P(data | p)
steps.explanation: Apply the same definition in the other direction and rearrange.
Replace P(p ∩ data) with the expression from Step 2.
P(data) is just a normalizing constant. Posterior ∝ likelihood × prior.

hints: What does P(A|B) equal? You just learned this definition.
P(data | p) = P(data ∩ p) / P(p). Rearrange to get P(data ∩ p).
Substitute your expression for P(p ∩ data) into Step 1.
P(data) is just a constant that makes probabilities sum to 1.

blocks.title: New Notation: Log-Likelihood
blocks.content_html: New notation: log means the natural logarithm (ln). We write ℓ(p) = log P(data|p) for the log-likelihood — the logarithm of the likelihood function. Taking logs turns products into sums, which makes calculus easier.

blocks.title: Derive the Width Formula (Part B)
blocks.prompt: Derive why the posterior width shrinks as 1/√N from the curvature of the log-likelihood.

steps.instruction: Take the log of the likelihood
steps.explanation: log(a × b) = log a + log b. log(a^n) = n log a.

steps.instruction: Find the second derivative at the peak
Read off the width from the curvature

hints: Use log(a × b) = log a + log b and log(a^n) = n log a.
d/dp log(p) = 1/p. d/dp log(1−p) = −1/(1−p). Set sum to zero.
Differentiate dℓ/dp one more time. d/dp(1/p) = −1/p².
Variance = 1/|curvature|. SD is the square root of variance.

blocks.title: Grid Approximation
blocks.prompt: Verify your derivations numerically using grid approximation in pure NumPy.
blocks.starter_code:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Define a grid of possible water fractions
p_grid = np.linspace(0, 1, 1000)
dp = p_grid[1] - p_grid[0]

# Define the prior: flat (uniform)
prior = np.ones(1000)

# Define the likelihood: 6 water in 9 tosses
k, n = 6, 9
likelihood = p_grid**k * (1 - p_grid)**(n - k)

# YOUR CODE: Compute posterior ∝ likelihood × prior
# Don't forget to normalize!
posterior = None

# Plot the posterior
plt.plot(p_grid, posterior)
plt.xlabel('Water fraction p')
plt.ylabel('Belief density')

# TASK 1: Find the peak (MAP estimate)
p_map = None
print(f'Peak: {p_map:.3f}')

# TASK 2: Compute the 89% interval width
cumulative = np.cumsum(posterior * dp)
lo = p_grid[np.searchsorted(cumulative, 0.055)]
hi = p_grid[np.searchsorted(cumulative, 0.945)]
width = hi - lo
print(f'89% interval width: {width:.3f}')
blocks.solution_code:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

p_grid = np.linspace(0, 1, 1000)
dp = p_grid[1] - p_grid[0]

prior = np.ones(1000)

k, n = 6, 9
likelihood = p_grid**k * (1 - p_grid)**(n - k)

posterior = likelihood * prior
posterior = posterior / (posterior.sum() * dp)
posterior_integral = np.sum(posterior * dp)

plt.plot(p_grid, posterior)
plt.xlabel('Water fraction p')
plt.ylabel('Belief density')

p_map = p_grid[np.argmax(posterior)]
print(f'Peak: {p_map:.3f}')

cumulative = np.cumsum(posterior * dp)
lo = p_grid[np.searchsorted(cumulative, 0.055)]
hi = p_grid[np.searchsorted(cumulative, 0.945)]
width = hi - lo
print(f'89% interval width: {width:.3f}')

blocks.title: Reconcile Your Understanding
blocks.prompt:
In your own words, write on the canvas:

1. Why more data makes beliefs sharper
2. Why the sharpening follows a square root law, not linear
3. What role the 'starting belief' plays when you have a lot of data

blocks.title: The Medical Test
blocks.content_html:
You just learned to update beliefs about one number (water fraction) from data. Here's where this matters in real life:

A medical test for a rare disease is 99% accurate. A patient tests positive. Most doctors believe there's a 99% chance the patient has the disease. They're wrong.

Suppose 1 in 10,000 people has the disease. Out of 10,000 people:

  • 1 truly sick person tests positive (99% accuracy)
  • 9,999 healthy people: 1% false positive rate → ~100 test positive
  • Total positive tests: 101. Only 1 is truly sick.
  • P(sick | positive) = 1/101 ≈ 1%, not 99%

Bayes' theorem handles this automatically. Without the prior, you'd tell 100 healthy people they're dying.

blocks.title: Practice: Different Data
blocks.template: {k} water in {n} tosses. Predict the 89% interval width.

blocks.title: Where This Goes
blocks.content_html:
You now have the fundamental tools of Bayesian inference:

  • Posterior ∝ Likelihood × Prior — Bayes' theorem
  • Width ∝ 1/√N — uncertainty shrinks with data
  • Grid approximation — numerical verification

Next lesson: What Can You Do with a Posterior? Point estimates, intervals, predictions, and the question that connects everything: when your model says one thing and reality says another, who do you believe?
